indicators.statement: Explain physical and emotional changes that Explain rites of passage characterise puberty and identify the moral
exemplars: lessons in them
Explain the meaning of puberty
It is the transition from childhood to adulthood. It occurs between the ages of 10 to 19 (WHO).
Discuss physical and emotional changes that occur at puberty Physical changes in girls at puberty
Growing of pubic hair
Menstruation
Breasts develop and increase in size
Hips widen
Change in height, etc. Physical changes in boys at puberty
Pubic hair
Break in voice
Sperms development
Penis grows bigger
Change in height etc. Emotional changes in both sexes
Curiosity
Adventurous
Conflict of identity (not child, not adult)
Mood swings

Development of the urge to have sex, etc. Learners to write their concerns relating to puberty anonymously and place them in a box. The teacher discusses these concerns in class to address any fears or misconceptions about puberty.

Marriage is a legal union between two adults (man and woman) globalisation on traditions, languages and and their families. cultures.

Types of Marriage Ceremonies (Rites). 6.1: Ability to effectively define goals o Customary/Traditional Marriage, towards solving a problem o Islamic marriage and 6.2: Availability to explain plans to attaining goals o Christian marriage. 5.6: Ability to set and maintain standards
Describe how marriage is performed in the three major religions. and values

Indigenous African Religion 6.3: Ability to manage time effectively Every ethnic group has its own way of performing the marriage 6.4: Ability to manage and resolve conflicts ceremony. However, there is a general pattern to follow
Expression of interest by the man’s family
Background investigation by both families
Giving of a list of items by the family of the female to the male’s family
Knocking ceremony
Fixing of date for the performance of the marriage ceremony
Payment of bride gift, etc.
Christianity Christian marriage goes through the following procedure
Expression of interest by the man’s family
Courtship and counselling
Traditional marriage rites
Wedding/blessing ceremony (exchange of vows and rings).

indicators.statement: Explain the need to obey God, parents and those in Identify and explain the authority importance of obeying
exemplars: authority
Learners to identify and discuss different reasons for obeying authority Reasons
It is a command from God (Ghanaian proverb: “Woe to a person who has no elder (authority)”; Deuteronomy. 6 and Qur’an 4:59)
We learn from the experience of people in authority
It makes us be law abiding
People in authority guide us to do the right and avoid the wrong
To promote peace in society
Obeying authority ensures progress, etc. Project Work Create a story to demonstrate the consequences of disobeying authority. Present it to class for discussion.

indicators.statement: Demonstrate how to apply the rules and regulations Identify and explain the from authority in daily life importance of obeying
exemplars: authority
Dramatise how to apply rules and regulations from authority
Home/family rules – performing household chores, respecting orders of parents and not bullying younger siblings, etc.
School rules and regulations – punctuality at and regularity to school, doing classwork and homework, sweeping the school compound, etc.
Communal rules and regulations – not littering our neighbourhood, respect for the elderly, involving oneself in communal work, etc.
Religious rules and regulations – going to church/mosque on time, dressing appropriately to religious meetings, paying church dues, paying zakat, and sadaqa, etc.

National rules and regulations – obeying traffic rules, singing the national anthem, showing respect to national symbols such as the flag and currency, punctuality to work, avoiding corruption, etc. Discuss lessons drawn out from the drama.

indicators.statement: Explain the term “money” and indicate its usefulness. 5.1: Ability to combine information use of money and ideas from several sources to reach a
exemplars: conclusion.

Look at pictures of money or real Ghanaian currency notes and coins, 5.6: Demonstrate a thorough understanding and explain the meaning of money and its usefulness. of a generalised concept or facts to task or Meaning of Money: situation.
Money is anything accepted as legal tender and can be used as medium of exchange. Usefulness of Money
used to promote God’s work
motivation to work
used to acquire our basic needs e.g. food, clothing, shelter, education
used to prepare for the future when we are not strong to work or we retire from work, e.g., social security
used to help the poor and needy in society, etc.

Money should not be acquired through begging unless very interaction and the expression of ideas and necessary. opinions in way that is sensitive to the feelings Christianity and background of others.
Hard work (Proverbs 14: 23-24; Proverbs 6:6-8; Colossians 3:2 3-24; Romans 11: 12-13)
Money should be acquired through good stewardship (Parable of the Talent Matthew 25-14-30)

Money should be acquired honestly and not from the exploitation of the poor and the weak (Proverbs 22:22-23; Proverbs 28:8) and gambling (Amos 4; Exodus 20: 17 and Luke 12: 15). Indigenous African Religion
Hard work
Prudence: Akan Proverb - “One cannot be feasting and at the same time become rich”.
Creativity brings wealth - ‘Poverty causes one to think creatively’
Good stewardship: “If one takes good care of another’s possession, one also gets possession”.
Money must not be acquired dishonestly, for example through robbery and the exploitation of the poor and the weak in society: If the wind tries to pick an object with speed, it leaves it behind.
